title: Learning in Financial Markets: Implications for Debt-Equity Conflicts
abstract: Financial markets reveal information that firm managers can utilize when making equity value-enhancing investment decisions. However, for firms with risky debt, such investments are not necessarily socially efficient. Despite this friction, we show that learning from prices improves investment efficiency. This effect is asymmetric, however, as investors learn less about projects that decrease the riskiness of cash flows: efficiency is lower for diversifying investments than for focusing (risk-increasing) investments. This also implies that investors’ endogenous learning further attenuates risk shifting but amplifies debt overhang. Our model provides a novel channel through which learning from financial markets affects agency frictions between stakeholders.

Our paper is motivated by two well-known observations. First, investors’ incentive to acquire information generally increases with the volatility of the asset’s underlying cash flows.1Second, in the presence of risky debt, firm managers prefer more volatile cash flows, ceteris paribus; however, such preferences may lead to socially inefficient investment decisions.2We argue that the growing feedback effect literature provides a novel connection between these two observations: investors’ private information, contained in secondary market prices, can serve as a valuable source of information for firm managers.3As a result, the riskiness of the firm’s cash flows is endogenously determined: the managers’ decision to invest (whichalterscash flow volatility) depends on investors’ decision to acquire information (which iscontingent oncash flow volatility). Thus, understanding how investors’ endogenous learning affects investment efficiency and the agency conflicts between firms’ stakeholders is both a natural and important issue for study.

The main challenge in studying such an interplay is that most noisy rational expectation equilibrium (REE) models, which are instrumental in analyzing this effect, rely on a linear pricing function. To accommodate the nonlinearity introduced by debt, the first part of the paper develops a novel REE that accommodates both (a) risky debt and (b) a feedback loop between the financial market and the firm’s investment decision. We then utilize this setting to study how managerial learning from prices and investors’ information choice affects investment efficiency.

We show that, even in the presence of risky debt, learning from prices generically crowds out some inefficient investments and crowds in some efficient investments, thereby improving investment efficiency. In particular, managerial learning from prices reduces the likelihood of an agency conflict. However, investors’ endogenous learning depends on the type of investment opportunity, creating an asymmetric effect: efficiency is higher (lower) for investments that increase (decrease) the riskiness of the firm’s cash flows. We show that this endogenous information acquisition further attenuates risk shifting, but amplifies the likelihood of observing debt overhang.

In contrast, managerial information acquisition depends on the riskiness of the project’s cash flows only; that is, her decision does not feature the asymmetry exhibited by investors. While the manager generally prefers investment projects featuring learnable uncertainty, that is, risky payoffs that are correlated with investors’ information, we show that she may choose to lower this uncertainty when the project decreases the riskiness of thefirm’scash flows. We also demonstrate how introducing traded debt can increase investment efficiency, in contrast to the literature emphasizing the role of monitoring. Finally, throughout our analysis, we highlight how investors’ endogenous information acquisition naturally leads to cross-sectional differences in investment policy and efficiency across firms (e.g., public vs. private).

We consider a three-date (two-period) model. At date zero, the firm owns an existing asset and has access to a potential investment. While the firm manager and investors share common prior beliefs about the investment, each (competitive) investor can acquire costly, private information about the project’s likelihood of success. At date zero, each investor chooses whether to become informed in anticipation of trading an equity claim in the next period. At date one, the firm manager must decide whether or not to invest in the new project, utilizing the information contained in the price of equity as well as her own private signal. Investors incorporate this “feedback channel” into their demand schedules and the manager’s decision is ultimately reflected in the price. At date two, the cash flows of any assets owned by the firm are realized and the proceeds are paid to existing debt and equity investors.

The extent to which the firm manager’s investment decision relies on the price depends on the quality of the information contained therein: as more investors acquire private information, the investment-to-price sensitivity increases. Note, though, that investors’ incentive to acquire information increases when the value of the traded claim is more sensitive to the private signal they receive. We consider investment projects that can amplify or attenuate the information sensitivity of equity, depending on the investment’s payoff distribution. This proves to be the crucial distinction between risk shifting and debt overhang. Projects subject to risk shifting (which are positively correlated with assets-in-place, or “focusing”) increase the information sensitivity of equity and, hence, the measure of informed investors. Projects that can induce debt overhang (negatively correlated with assets-in-place, or “diversifying”) cause both to fall. Thus, ex ante variation in project characteristics leads to endogenous variation in the measure of informed investors. This, in turn, generates ex post variation in the likelihood that the manager’s investment decision is efficient.

We show that learning from prices always improves investment efficiency, even in the presence of potential agency conflicts. Though the manager’s investment threshold is inefficient, her objective is to invest in those states when the equity return (and therefore, investment return) is positive. By conditioning on the price, she is more likely to identify these states, and so ex post social efficiency increases, even though the manager invests only for the benefit of equity holders. Because the impact of learning from the price of equity is increasing in the measure of informed investors, investment efficiency is lower for diversifying projects relative to focusing projects. This asymmetric effect also implies that firms with access to risk shifting projects are more likely to successfully identify when (and when not) to invest than firms with projects that have the potential to exhibit debt overhang.

We then consider the impact of managerial learning from prices on the observation of agency conflicts by an econometrician. When the price reveals “good news” about the investment payoff, the manager is more likely to pursue the investment, while an econometrician, who also observes the price of equity, is more likely to infer that the decision to invest was ex ante efficient. Analogously, when the price reveals “bad news” about the investment payoff, the manager is less likely to invest, while the econometrician is more likely to agree that the investment was ex ante inefficient.

This implies, however, that endogenous learning by investors reduces the likelihood of risk shifting. Because such projects are positively correlated with assets-in-place, the potential adoption of such projects encourages information acquisition by investors. This increases price informativeness, making it more likely that either good or bad news is revealed. As a result, it is less likely that the econometrician views the manager’s decision as risk shifting. On the other hand, when faced with the prospect of debt overhang, the manager is more likely to forgo projects with positive expected value due to the endogenous reduction in informed investors such projects engender. Investments in such projects (which are negatively correlated with assets-in-place) reduce the information sensitivity of equity and, hence, the measure of informed investors. The resultant decline in price informativeness decreases the probability that either efficient investment is crowded in or inefficient investment is crowded out. Thus, it is more likely that the firm’s investment decision is identified as socially inefficient.

To highlight the unique asymmetry introduced by investor learning, we extend our benchmark model to allow for managerial information acquisition. We show that the endogenous precision of the manager’s signal increases with the information sensitivity (i.e., riskiness) of theproject. As a result, and in contrast to investors’ endogenous learning, managerial information acquisition increases investment efficiency symmetrically for diversifying and focusing investments.

We also extend the model to allow the manager to choose the potential investment project in anticipation of the impact this will have on investors’ incentive to acquire information. The value of any potential investment can be decomposed into the expected payoff (given the manager’s information) plus the option value created when an informative price alters the manager’s investment decision. Consistent with the specification of our baseline model, the manager generally prefers risky investments that covary with investors’ information: this maximizes the option value. However, when the potential investments are diversifying, riskier projects reduce the measure of informed investors. As a result of this endogenous decline in price information, and in contrast to standard intuition, the option value of such projects may be decreasing in their risk.

Finally, we show that debt investors’ incentive to acquire information mirrors that of equity investors: focusing (diversifying) investments increase (decrease) the information-sensitivity of debt, all else equal. As a result, introducing traded debt can amplify the effect of managerial learning from prices. While the existing literature highlights the important role of monitoring when comparing bank debt, for example, to traded debt, our analysis suggests that the information contained in the secondary debt market may exert a countervailing force.

Our model suggests that this difference in the prevalence of risk shifting and debt overhang is more likely to arise when the firm has publicly, not privately held equity. This is consistent with the empirical literature, which has thus far found evidence consistent with debt overhang, for example,Wittry (2021)andHennessy (2004), but little support for risk shifting, for example,Andrade and Kaplan (1998)andGilje (2016), in publicly traded firms. Notably, our paper provides a single, novel channel through which such a disparity arises. Moreover, our model’s focus on learning from prices naturally suggests that, all else equal, public firms should be more responsive to investment opportunities, as inGilje and Taillard (2016), which can lead to differences in investment behavior for public versus private firms, as documented inFeldman, Kawano, Patel, Rao, Stevens, and Edgerton (2018).

Intuitively, our results will be more pronounced in settings where investors have access to incremental, payoff-relevant information and can endogenously choose whether or not to become informed. This is consistent with, for example,Luo (2005), who provides evidence that an acquisition is more likely to be canceled if the market reacts negatively, particularly in cases in which learning is more probable. Our results imply that investment-to-price sensitivity should be (a) higher when firm managers have the opportunity to indulge in risk shifting or have access to risk-increasing investments but (b) lower in the presence of risk-decreasing projects that can give rise to debt overhang. More generally, the model predicts that price informativeness and, hence, investment efficiency, should be lower for diversifying investments, consistent withSchoar (2002). Finally, because of their impact on the information-sensitivity of traded claims, our model predicts that firm characteristics, for example, riskiness of assets-in-place, can lead to cross-sectional differences in investment efficiency even when these firms face identical investment opportunities.

At its core, our model emphasizes the important role played by financial markets in aggregating and disseminating information, followingGrossman (1976),Grossman and Stiglitz (1980),Hellwig (1980), andDiamond and Verrecchia (1981). Our emphasis is on the impact this information has on firm decision-making, as in the “feedback literature” comprehensively surveyed byBond, Edmans, and Goldstein (2012).4,5As inGoldstein, Ozdenoren, and Yuan (2013)andDow, Goldstein, and Guembel (2017), investors in our model act competitively; the private information they possess is impounded into the price through their trading activity in a nonstrategic manner.6While in many of these settings the manager is uninformed, we assume that the firm manager has access to a noisy, private signal but that the price is incrementally informative as in, for example,Bond and Goldstein (2015). LikeBond, Goldstein, and Prescott (2009), our results focus on the differential impact when the decisionmaker’s action can increase or decrease the sensitivity of the price to the underlying state of the world. In contrast to these papers, however, we consider a setting in which the firm has risky debt, which allows us to analyze the impact of this feedback channel on the prevalence of agency conflicts.

In the presence of risky debt,Myers (1977)argued that equity holders may forgo investing in projects with positive returns (when gains accrue to debt holders), whileJensen and Meckling (1976)suggested that equity holders may prefer to invest in risky projects, even if inefficient (when losses are borne by debt holders). We show that when firm managers learn from prices both activities are reduced; however, once we account for investors’ endogenous learning, we show that this should affect risk shifting more than debt overhang. The latter pattern is consistent with the empirical literature, includingWittry (2021)andHennessy (2004), who find evidence of debt overhang, whileAndrade and Kaplan (1998),Rauh (2008), andGilje (2016)find little evidence for risk shifting. We discuss this evidence in more detail in Section 5.

The existing theoretical literature has suggested other explanations for why risk shifting may not arise. In dynamic settings, bothDiamond (1989)andHirshleifer and Thakor (1992)consider the impact of reputational concerns on investment decisions. Similarly,Almeida, Campello, and Weisbach (2011)suggest that firms may reduce risk today so that positive net present value (NPV) projects can be funded in the future. We analyze a static setting and emphasize the role that contemporaneous prices (instead of access to future opportunities) can play in affecting the observation of agency problems. In contrast to the existing literature, however, our model also predicts an asymmetric impact on risk shifting relative to debt overhang.

Finally, our model focuses on the conflict between bond holders and equity holders; as a result, and unlike standard REE models in which prices and cash flows are linear, our framework must allow for nonlinear claims (i.e., debt and equity). As such, it is most closely related toDavis (2019),Albagli, Hellwig, and Tsyvinski (2015,2017), andChabakauri, Yuan, and Zachariadis (2022).Albagli, Hellwig, and Tsyvinski (2017)explores the implications of a systematic wedge between prices and cash flow expectations for corporate risk-taking and investment. In contrast, and similar toDavis (2019), our analysis highlights the impact of endogenous investor information acquisition; however, the focus ofDavis (2019)is the firm’s optimal issuance policy (post-investment), while we examine the firm’s investment decision. Moreover, our extension allows for feedback between the manger’s investment decision and the price, a featureDavis (2019)does not consider.

1.1 Discussion of assumptions

In the benchmark model, we assume the manager is endowed with a private signal of fixed precision and a single investment project. InAppendix B.1, we allow the manager to choose the precision of her signal and analyze how this choice interacts with investors’ information acquisition decision and the resultant impact on our main results. In addition to its empirical relevance, the assumption that the manager cannot trade allows us to focus on the manager’s incentive to acquire information for the firm’s investment decision, not trading purposes.11

InAppendix B.2, we explore how the manager would choose from a set of projects, knowing that this choice would influence the investors’ information choice and, therefore, the information contained in prices. We further generalize this analysis by examining a setting in which the state-dependent investment payoff is drawn from a nondegenerate distribution. We show that an alternative interpretation of the benchmark model is thatyHandyLmeasures the extent to which the investment project is correlated with theinformationabout the assets-in-place.

Throughout our main analysis, we assume that the firm’s existing debt is not traded. Such debt may have been previously issued to finance the firm’s assets-in-place and held in private, for example, by a bank.12InAppendix B.3, we relax this assumption and show that when such debt claims are traded, debt investors’ incentive to learn mirrors the decision of equity investors, amplifying our main results. Finally, markets are incomplete in our setting in the sense that investors cannot trade separate claims to the firms’ asset-in-place and the investment projects. This is a natural assumption in our setting: if the firm does not pursue the investment opportunity, the investment payoff may never be realized (e.g., the payoff may be driven by synergies between the firm and a potential acquisition target.)

2.2 Information acquisition equilibrium (date-0)

At date zero, each investor can pay a fixed cost to acquire a private signal about the true state of the world. The value of acquiring this signal depends on the sensitivity of the investor’s valuation to his belief about the realized state, that is,

∂ E ( F i ) ∂ q ( F i ) = E H ( p E ) − E L ( p E ) ≡ Δ E ( p E ) . (18)

We will refer toΔE(pE)as theinformation-sensitivity of equity. Intuitively, ifΔE(pE)were zero for allpE, then the value of an equity claim would not depend on the investor’s information.

Investors decide whether to acquire information by comparing the fixed cost,c, to the increase in expected utility from observing private signalsi. Given the nature of the information technology, two potential types of equilibria are possible: corner solutions (in which either all investors or no investors become informed) or an interior equilibrium featuring both informed and uninformed investors. In the latter, investorimust be indifferent between (a) payingcand trading on both a private signal,si, and the price,pE, and (b) trading only on the information contained in the pricepE, that is,

︸ ︸ E [ d ( { s i , p E } ) ( E ( { s i , p E } ) − p E ) ︸ informed trading gains ] − c = E [ d ( { p E } ) ( E ( { p E } ) − p E ) ︸ uninformed trading gains ] . (19)

Recall that investors’ valuations differ only in their beliefs about the likelihood of each state. Letq^(pE)denote the belief of the marginal investor when the price ispE. Then, the indifference condition can be rewritten so that in an interior equilibrium:

E [ d ( { s i , p E } ) ( q ( { s i , p E } ) − q ^ { p E } ) ) Δ E ( p E ) ] − c = E [ d ( { p E } ) ( q ( { p E } ) − q ^ ( { p E } ) ) Δ E ( p E ) ] . (20)

Investors’ expected trading gains depend on not only the difference in beliefs,q(Fi)−q^(pE)⁠, but also the expected difference in the equity payoff across the two states,ΔE(pE)⁠. Thus, the information-sensitivity of equity plays a key role in determining the measure of informed investors,λ, which, in turn, determines the likelihood that the price is informative.

Proposition 2. If c > c ^ ⁠ , defined in Equation (A.85) , then λ = 0. Otherwise,

λ ρ λ = 1 − c ( ρ − 1 2 ) Δ E ( p u ) < 1. (21)

Ifc<c^⁠, the measure of informed investors is decreasing in the cost of information (c) and increasing in both the precision of the private signal (ρ) and the information-sensitivity of equity when the price is uninformative (⁠ΔE(pu)⁠).

In the proof of the proposition, we show that the additional trading gains earned by informed investors (in expectation) arise when the marginal investor is uninformed, that is, ifpE=pu. As we showed earlier, when the marginal investor is uninformed, the price is uninformative. Thus, the difference between the informed investor’s belief about the likelihood of the high state and the marginal investor’s belief isρ(ρ−12)⁠, which reflects their information advantage relative to the marginal investor.16This information advantage is scaled by the difference in the payoff across the two states, that is, the information-sensitivity of equity. Thus, in any interior equilibrium, the measure of informed investors is increasing in (a) informed investors’ information advantage,ρ(ρ−12)⁠, as well as (b) the information-sensitivity of equity when the price is uninformative,ΔE(pu)⁠.

If the cost of acquiring a private signal is too high, no investors choose to do so andλ= 0. However, it is never the case that all investors choose to acquire information and so, in equilibrium,λis always less than one. The advantage of being informed arises when the price is set by an uninformed investor: if there were no uninformed investors, there would be no incentive to pay a cost to become informed. As a result,λ= 1 cannot be an equilibrium.

Given the dependence ofλon the information-sensitivity of equity, we conclude this section by analyzing the impact of investment onΔE(pu)⁠. It is straightforward to rewrite the information-sensitivity, given the definition ofσ,γ, as

σ ω γ ︸ ω Δ E ( p u ​ ) = E H , N I − E L , N I + σ [ ​ ​ ​ P [ d m = I | ω = H , p u ] ( 1 + γ ) ︸ > 0 + P [ d m = I | ω = L , p u ] ​ ​ ​ ] ​ . (22)

The first term,EH,NI−EL,NI≡ΔE0⁠, captures the information sensitivity of the firm’s assets-in-place. The second piece captures the impact of the investment project. When the manager invests in a project that is positively correlated with the firm’s assets-in-place (i.e.,σσ>0⁠), the firm’s value is increasingly dependent on the realization of the underlying state. Ifσσ>0⁠, thenyH>0>yL⁠: investment widens the gap between the firm’s value in the high and low state and, as a result, the information sensitivity of equity increases. In contrast, when the project is negatively correlated with assets-in-place (i.e.,σσ<0⁠), investing serves as a hedge against the state-specific risk the firm faces. As a result, the value of an equity claim is less sensitive to investors’ beliefs and the information-sensitivity falls. Intuitively, and as seen in(22), these effects are magnified the more likely the manager is to invest, that is, asP[dm=I|pu]increases. We summarize these results in the following lemma.

Lemma 2. The information-sensitivity of equity, Δ E ( p u ) ⁠ , and, hence, λ , is

increasing inσas long as the manager invests whenpE=pu(i.e., ifP[dm=I|pu]≠0⁠);increasing in the information-sensitivity of assets-in-place,EH,NI−EL,NI⁠.

This result is reminiscent of the findings ofDow, Goldstein, and Guembel (2017). In their paper, information acquisition can exhibit complementarity across investors: as more investors become informed, it becomes more likely that the manager invests, which increases the value of being informed. Similarly, we find that whenσσ>0⁠, an increase in the manager’s likelihood of investment encourages more investors to become informed. There are, however, two distinguishing features. First, in our setting the firm has (risky) assets-in-place so that an investment project that is negatively correlated (i.e.,σσ<0⁠) leads to the opposite result: increased investment lowers the information-sensitivity, which discourages information acquisition. Second, in our setting, no such complementarity can arise because the incentive to acquire information depends on the manager’s investment policy when the price is uninformative.17
article.3 Implications for Investment:
3.1 Investment efficiency

We consider first the real impact of price information utilizing a standardized measure ofinvestment efficiency,I⁠, where

I ≡ P [ y > 0 | d m ( { p E , s m } ) = I ] + P [ y < 0 | d m ( { p E , s m } ) = N I ] . (23)

This measure of investment efficiency captures the likelihood that the manager’s investment decision is ex post socially-efficient.18Importantly,Icaptures the incremental impact of the manager’s endogenous information set without the distorting influence of the investment project’s scale. That is, holding fixed the value of the potential investment opportunity, an increase inIreflects a higher, positive correlation between investment and future firm performance.19

We first show that utilizing the information in the price of equity can both crowd-in efficient investments and crowd-out the inefficient, as summarized by the following proposition.

Proposition 3. Investment efficiency ( ⁠ I ⁠ ) is increasing in the precision of investors’ and manager’s information ( ρ and ρ m ) and the measure of informed investors ( λ ), sometimes strictly.

For intuition, consider the setting in whichσσ>0⁠. When the price is uninformative, the manager’s decision to invest only depends on her private signal,sm. Suppose thatρmandKare such that she invests whenp=puonly if her private signal indicates the high state is likely (i.e., ifsm=h).20Observing “good news” in the financial market (pE=ph) can encourage investment when the manager’s private signal is pessimistic (sm=l) if the signal she extracts from the price is sufficiently precise, that is, ifρis sufficiently high such thatq({sm=l,ph})>K⁠. Similarly, observing “bad news” in the market (pE=pl) can discourage investment when the manager’s private signal is optimistic (sm=h) as long as the precision of the price information is high enough to push the manager’s belief,q({sm=h,pl})⁠, below the investment threshold. We refer to these as thecrowding-inandcrowding-outeffect of learning from prices, respectively.

In expectation, these changes in the manager’s investment decision increase investment efficiency. Importantly, this is true whether the firm’s debt is risk-free or risky, that is, even if the manager’s investment threshold,K, is inefficient due to the presence of risky debt. To see why, note that the manager’s objective is to correctly identify the state in which the investment return (and therefore, equity return) is positive. When she conditions on a more informative price, she is more likely to identify the true state and so ex post efficiency increases regardless of the investment threshold she utilizes. Thus, even though the manager utilizes the price information for the benefit of equity holders only, when more investors are informed (increasedλ) with more precise private information (increasedρ), realized investment efficiency increases.

As a result, investors’ information impacts the manager’s decision to invest through two, distinct channels. First, if the precision,ρ, of investors’ private signals increases then, when the price is informative, the manager isalsoable to extract a more precise signal from the price. If this pushes the manager’s posterior belief beyond her investment threshold, that is, if this shifts the manager’s investment decision, then it is more likely that the true state is identified and efficiency increases (on average). Second, if the measure of informed investors,λ, rises, then the likelihood that the price is informative increases, which increases, in turn, the likelihood an efficient (inefficient) project is crowded-in (crowded-out).

The measure of informed investors, however, is endogenous: our derived expression for the equilibriumλ(given byequation 21), implies that investment efficiency is decreasing in investors’ cost of learning,c, but increasing in bothΔE(pu)andρ. Recall that the information-sensitivity of equity,ΔE(pu)⁠, depends on both the characteristics of the assets-in-place as well as the potential investment. We begin with the former.

Corollary 1. Investment efficiency ( ⁠ I ⁠ ) is increasing in the information sensitivity of the firm’s assets-in-place, E H , N I − E L , N I ⁠ .

Corollary 1 suggests that, holding fixed the investment characteristics, investment efficiency is higher for firms with riskier assets-in-place. Specifically, increased uncertainty about the firm’s existing assets increases investors’ incentive to acquire information that, in turn, provides more information to the manager about the investment project.21This suggests that, even when given access to the same investment, two otherwise identical firms may exhibit different levels of investment efficiency, due to differences in the information-sensitivity of their assets-in-place.

Corollary 2. Investment efficiency ( ⁠ I ⁠ ) is increasing in σ if the P [ d m = I | p u ] is nonzero.

Corollary 2 implies that investment efficiency will be higher when the firm’s potential projects are more positively correlated with their assets-in-place. As a result, investments thatincreasefirm risk, all else equal, are more likely to be successfully identified, while investments that lower firm risk will be less valuable in expectation. Alternatively, one can view changes inσas reflecting the degree to which the firm is either specializing (⁠σσ>0⁠) or diversifying (⁠σσ<0⁠). Our results suggest that, when firms rely on the financial market for information, specialization is rewarded over diversification, all else equal. We discuss how this relates to the literature on firm diversification in Section 5. Moreover, the impact of changes inσdepend on the probability of investment when the price is uninformative, as seen inEquation (22). This suggests that projects with the highest expected payoff (in theabsenceof a financial market), that is, those with the highest probability of investment whenp=pu, may not be the most valuable when the manager can learn from prices. InAppendix B.2, we explore a related idea in a setting in which the manager can choose which project to pursue.

3.2 Agency conflicts

LetNPV(F)denotes the net present value of the investment given any information setF⁠, i.e.,

NPV ( F ) ≡ q ( F ) y H + ( 1 − q ( F ) ) y L . (24)

This implies that, ifσσ>0⁠, it is socially efficient to invest as long asq(F)>−yLyH−yL≡K0,while ifσσ<0⁠, the inequality flips: investment is efficient ifq(F)<K0⁠.

In the presence of risky debt, a portion of the gains and losses from investment accrue to debt-holders. This creates a wedge between the socially efficient investment threshold and the manager’s investment threshold, that is,K0≠K⁠. This wedge gives rise to two sources of conflict between debt and equity holders: risk shifting, as inJensen and Meckling (1976)and debt overhang, as inMyers (1977). We begin by establishing under what conditions such conflicts can arise in our model.

Lemma 3. Risk shifting is only possible when σ σ > 0 ⁠ . Debt overhang is only possible when σ σ < 0 ⁠ .

Risky debt distorts the manager’s investment threshold,K, downward relative to the socially-efficient threshold, so thatK0>K⁠. This implies that whenσσ>0⁠, the manager will choose to invest in a project when

K 0 > q ( F m ) > K . (25)

This is an example ofrisk shifting: though the expected value of the investment is negative (becauseK0>q(Fm)⁠), the manager chooses to invest because it increases the expected equity payoff (becauseq(Fm)>K⁠). Intuitively, if the project is positively correlated with existing assets, equity holders capture relatively more of the gain and bear less of the loss, which can induce the manager to make an inefficient investment (from the perspective of a social planner). In contrast, debt overhang cannot arise whenσσ>0⁠: any project the manager chooses to forgo because her posterior belief falls belowKis also ex ante inefficient.

Whenσσ<0⁠, then the manager will choosenot to investin a project under the same condition(25): ifq(Fm)>K⁠, then the likelihood of the low state is too low to justify investment on equity holders’ behalf. However, choosing such an investment would be socially efficient sinceK0>q(Fm)and, as such, this is an example ofdebt overhang. When the project is negatively correlated with assets-in-place, debt-holders capture more of the gain (in the low state) and bear less of the loss (in the high state). For this reason, however, risk shifting cannot arise whenσσ<0since any project the manager takes (becauseq(Fm)<K⁠) is efficient as well.

We aim to understand how managerial learning from prices is affected by the nature of the potential agency conflict. To isolate the impact of the agency conflict across different projects, we will focus on a setting in which the only thing that varies is the ex ante investment value.

Lemma 4. If γ = 0, then the ex ante return on the investment is decreasing in σ , σ ∂ NPV ( F 0 ) ∂ σ < 0 ⁠ , where F 0 denotes agents’ date-zero beliefs.

In what follows, we utilizeσ|σ|as a proxy for the intensity of the potential agency conflict. Whenγis zero, varyingσdoes not change the ex ante investment return to equity-holders: it is fixed at zero. Whenσis positive, however, the net present value of the investment must be negative. Equity holders capture a larger fraction of the cash flows in the high state, which implies that|yH|must be less than|yL|⁠. Whenσσ>0⁠, higherσcaptures increasingly inefficient examples of risk shifting since, from the social planner’s perspective, the ex ante value of the investment is falling (by Lemma 4). Similarly, whenσis negative, the net present value of the investment must be positive: equity holders capture a smaller fraction of the cash flows in the low state and so|yL|>|yH|⁠. Thus, ifσσ<0⁠, lowerσcaptures increasingly inefficient examples of debt overhang, since the ex ante value of a forgone project is increasingly positive.

Comparing across different projects whenγ= 0 has an additional benefit: it implies that conditioning on the price can both crowd-in and crowd-out investment by the manager. In the presence of agency conflicts, this is important as it allows us to capture both sides of the impact of managerial learning from prices.

With these motivations in mind, we characterize the impact of feedback on observed agency conflicts under the assumption thatγ= 0 in what follows. We demonstrate the robustness of our results under two alternative assumptions in theonline appendix. In particular, we analyze both the impact of learning whenγγ≠0⁠, as well as a complementary setting in which the ex ante value of the investment proxies for the agency conflict.

Corollary 3. As the potential investment loss from risk shifting grows (an increase in σ σ > 0 ⁠ ), investment efficiency ( ⁠ I ⁠ ) increases. As the potential forgone gain from debt overhang grows (a decrease in σ σ < 0 ⁠ ), investment efficiency ( ⁠ I ⁠ ) decreases.

Corollary 3 implies that firms with access to risk shifting projects are more likely to successfully identify when (and when not) to invest than firms with projects that have the potential to exhibit debt overhang. This effect is driven by the impact each type of project has on the measure of informed investors and, hence, price informativeness. Moreover, the worst examples of risk shifting, that is, those projects with the lowest ex ante value, lead to the lowest degree of ex post investment inefficiency because they maximize investor learning. In contrast, the worst examples of debt overhang, that is, those projects with the highest ex ante value, are most likely to be inefficiently discarded because they discourage information acquisition by the investor.

Our measure of real efficiency,I⁠, conditions on the realized investment payoff. However, debt-equity conflicts arise due to disagreements about the manager’sex antedecision. To account for this, we want to measure how often the manager is observed engaging in either risk shifting or debt overhang. Specifically, such metrics respect the segmentation of information and are based only on what an econometrician can observe when the manager makes her decision. We write the econometrician’s information set asFe≡{pE,dm}⁠.22

We consider two such metrics. The first is the unconditional probability that the manager makes a socially inefficient investment decision (the intensity of the agency conflict):

σ P 1 ( σ ) ≡ P [ K 0 > q ( F e ) > K ] . (26)

The second measure is the conditional probability that an investment decision is inefficient:

σ P 2 ( σ ) ≡ P ( K 0 > q ( F e ) > K ) P ( q ( F e ) > K ) . (27)

Whenσσσ>0,P1(σ)is the likelihood that the firm manager will take a risk shifting investment given her information, whileσP2(σ)is the likelihood that an investment chosen is inefficient. Whenσσσ<0,P1(σ)is the ex ante likelihood that the manager will engage in debt overhang by choosing not to invest;σP2(σ)is the probability that a foregone investment is socially efficient.

Lemma 5. Learning from a more informative equity price reduces the likelihood of an agency conflict, that is, P 1 and P 2 are decreasing in λ and ρ .

When an informative price (i.e.,p∈{pl,ph}⁠) is realized, the manager and econometrician update their beliefs about the likelihood of the true state in the same direction. Thus, asλincreases, the investment decision islesslikely to be viewed as inefficient.λ, however, is endogenous. Lemma 5 implies that a reduction in the cost of acquiring information or an increase in the information-sensitivity of assets-in-place, both of which increaseλ, reduce bothP1 andP2. An increase in the precision of investors’ signals,ρ, also increasesλ, but affectsP1 andP2 through a second channel as well. Specifically, asρincreases, the beliefs of the manager and econometrician converge, so that it is more likely that when the manager’s belief exceedsK, that the econometrician’s belief exceedsK0, reducing observed agency conflicts.

Importantly, we note that the absence of a financial market is equivalent to a setting in which all investors are uninformed (i.e.,λ= 0). Thus, Lemma 5 implies that access to the information in prices reduces the observation ofbothtypes of agency conflict.

Proposition 4. Investors’ endogenous information acquisition attenuates the observation of risk shifting but amplifies the observation of debt overhang, that is, P 1 and P 2 are strictly decreasing in σ for σ σ σ σ ∈ ( σ l , σ h ] ⁠ , while for σ σ σ > σ h or σ σ σ ≤ σ l ⁠ , both P1 and P2 are constant.

Proposition 4 implies that, endogenous learning by investors further reduces the observation of risk shifting (⁠σσ>0⁠) but increases the observation of debt overhang (⁠σσ<0⁠). This effect is driven by the impact such projects have on the information sensitivity and, hence, the likelihood that the econometrician observes an informative price. However, in our setting, the price of equity takes only three discrete values, and the econometrician’s belief about the likelihood of the high state is bounded both above and below. As a result, forσsufficiently high or low (i.e.,σσσ>σhandσσσ≤σl⁠), investment is always viewed as efficient (or inefficient) regardless of the price observed.23

article.4 Extensions and Robustness:
The specific assumptions we make are for analytic tractability and to highlight the underlying mechanism in the clearest manner. InAppendix B, we extend our results along several important dimensions, while in theonline appendix, we establish the robustness of our results to several alternative specifications. In this section, we summarize the insights from this analysis, with particular focus paid to the role of investors’ endogenous information acquisition.

In Section B.1, we allow the manager to choose the precision of her private signal. Unlike investors, we show that her choice depends not onσ(which determines the information sensitivity of equity) but onσ|σ|(which determines the information sensitivity of the project.) This is because the manager is endowed with an equity claim through the contract implied by her objective function: the value of her investment decision and hence, her private information, only depends on the incremental information sensitivity of the project. In contrast, investors are choosing whether to purchase an equity claim and so choose to learn based on the combined information sensitivity of assets-in-place and the potential investment.

In the benchmark model, the manager is endowed with a single investment opportunity. In Section B.2, we allow her to choose the project from a set of potential investments before investors choose whether to become informed. In Section B.2.1, the manager chooses the distribution of cash flows within each state, while in Section B.2.2, the manager chooses the distribution of cash flows across each state. In both cases, we hold fixed the ex ante payoff of the project. We show that the manager generally prefers projects that covary more (i.e., largeσ|σ|⁠) with investors’ information: this increases the implicit option value of learning from the price of equity. However, she would choose to avoid projects withσσ≪0since she internalizes the reduction in investors’ incentive to become informed (which reduces the option value, all else equal.)

In Section B.3, we introduce a debt market and allow the manager to learn from both the price of debt and the price of equity. We show that projects that increase the information sensitivity of equity also increase the information-sensitivity of debt. As a result, our main results extend to settings with traded debt as well. We find that an increase in firm leverage can increase or decrease the measure of informed investors, however, and that the impact depends on both the source of the leverage shock and whether or not the firm has traded debt.

We take as given that the manager chooses to maximize the terminal value of an equity (i.e., she is focused on the firm’s long-term value). One alternative is that the manager instead chooses to invest to maximize the date one equity price (i.e., existing equity holders are more short-term oriented). It is straightforward to show that our results are robust to any contract which is a linear combination of short-term and long-term incentives: under both objectives, the expected information sensitivity of equity responds similarly to a change inσ. Alternatively, as shown inStrobl (2014), committing to an alternative investment threshold can be optimal in the presence of endogenous information acquisition.26In the context of our model, a contract that induces the manager to invest in projects that lower the value of equity may be optimal: ifσσ>0⁠, such investments raise the information-sensitivity of equity, which increases the measure of informed investors and, hence, price informativeness. However, when the investment project decreases the information-sensitivity (⁠σσ<0⁠), the opposite may be true: she may find it optimal to commit tonotinvest in a project that she believes increases the expected equity payoff because such investments lower price informativeness.27

Finally, we note that the manager and investors hold common prior beliefs about the distribution of cash flows in our model. It is reasonable, however, to believe that while investors are better informed about external conditions, including the state of the macroeconomy or industry trends (all reflected in their beliefs about the probability of each state), the manager may possess more “firm-specific” or internal information (contained in his beliefs about the value of assets-in-place). Such information can shift the manager’s investment threshold,K, which can subsequently increase (or decrease) the likelihood of investment. However, as long as the distribution of the manager’s signal is common knowledge, the existence of our financial market equilibrium is unaffected since all investors share common beliefs regarding theωP[dm=I|ω,pE]⁠. Moreover, while access to such private information can increase or decreaseλ, the measure of informed investors (through its impact onP[dm=I|pu]⁠),λis still increasing inσ.
article.5 Additional Empirical Implications:
The main predictions of our model focused on investment efficiency and the likelihood of observing debt-equity conflicts. The channels underlying these results, however, suggest potential tests for the model’s proposed mechanism.

First, our model predicts that price informativeness and investment efficiency will be higher when the firm has access to “focusing” projects (or those that have the potential for risk shifting) due to the increased measure of informed investors. In contrast, price informativeness and investment efficiency will be lower when the firm’s potential investments are diversifying (or can give rise to debt overhang). Testing this prediction requires a measure of investment opportunities, a measure of price informativeness and investment efficiency. One possibility is to explore price informativeness after the announcement of a potentially risky project versus one that is more likely to reduce uncertainty. Alternatively, one way to implement our measure of investment efficiency empirically is to examine if investment is associated with higher future performance after controlling for ex ante investment opportunities.Jayaraman and Wu (2020)use a closely related measure and argue that firm’s capex adjustments (defined as capital expenditures minus “forecasts of capex”), which are due to market reactions (i.e., because of the feedback effect), correlate with higher future performance. Moreover, they show that this response is stronger when investors are more informed.

Second, our model predicts that managerial learning from prices increases for risk-increasing investments and decreases for risk-decreasing investments. A widely used empirical measure of managerial learning from prices is the investment-to-price sensitivity. For example, whenγ= 0, this is

λ Cov ( d m , p ) = λ ( p h − p u ) 4 . (28)

This is increasing inλ, sinceph>pu, which implies that the investment-to-price sensitivity should exhibit the same pattern as price informativeness.

Third, the potential importance of agency conflicts is heightened when firms experience financial distress. While empirically identifying the impact of leverage can be difficult due to endogeneity concerns,Gilje (2016)circumvents this by analyzing the investment behavior of public oil and gas firms that experience exogenous, negative shocks to their assets-in-place. The author finds that, rather than engaging in risk shifting, such firms tend to invest in lower-risk projects, which he suggests may be due to the firm’s need to access capital markets in the short term, as inDiamond (1989).28In our static model, there is no such exogenous preference for low-risk projects however, as we describe inAppendix B.3, such shocks to assets-in-place and hence leverage can endogenously increase the information-sensitivity of assets-in-place, denoted byΔE0⁠. This increases the measure of informed investors (i.e.,λ∂λ∂ΔE0>0⁠), especially forlow-risk projects, sinceλσ∂2λ∂σ∂ΔE0≤0⁠.29The relative increase inλimplies that the (relative) value of low-risk projects increases (see, e.g.,(B.9)), making them a more attractive option for the firm. This suggests that, when firms can choose from multiple projects, such leverage shocks can lead to fewer high-risk (or risk shifting) investments and more low-risk (or risk-reducing) investments.30

Fourth, our model’s focus on managerial learning from security prices naturally suggests differences in investment outcomes for public versus private firms. Much of the existing literature on investment differences has emphasized the trade-off between the agency costs that can arise from dispersed and potentially transient public ownership, for example,Drobetz, Janzen, and Meier (2019)and the increased access to capital available to publicly traded firms, for example,Gilje and Taillard (2016). In our analysis, the key difference is that publicly traded firms have access to better information about potential investments, increasing both investment efficiency and the investment-to-price sensitivity. For example,Gilje and Taillard (2016)find that public firms are more responsive to investment opportunities. Moreover, our model implies that the magnitude of this benefit is higher (lower) when the investment opportunity set consists of specializing (diversifying) projects. This may potentially explain the findings ofFeldman, Kawano, Patel, Rao, Stevens, and Edgerton (2018), who show that public firms invest more, especially in riskier projects (e.g., R&D). The differences in investment efficiency across project types may also help to explain the cross-sectional differences documented byAsker, Farre-Mensa, and Ljungqvist (2014)in private versus public investment efficiency across industries.

While our results suggest that, all else equal, going public lowers the observed incidence of both debt overhang and risk shifting, the relative impact depends on the type of agency conflict. Specifically, when a private firm exhibits risk shifting, going public can have a large impact given the increased information found in prices. This may help reconcile the observation of risk shifting in venture capital firms, as inDenes (2017), while the existing literature has largely failed to detect such actions in public firms, even those experiencing financial distress as pointed out byAndrade and Kaplan (1998)andGilje (2016).31In contrast, when a private firm exhibits debt overhang, going public may have a more modest impact, which may explain why such behavior is documented in public firms by bothHennessy (2004)andWittry (2021).

Fifth, an extensive theoretical literature analyzes the impact of diversification on firm value. While some papers have highlighted the role of asymmetric information within the firm, for example,Harris, Kriebel, and Raviv (1982), our model highlights the importance of information production outside of the firm. Specifically, when the project (or potential acquisition target) is “focusing,” that is, more correlated with existing assets or divisions, security prices are more informative and, as a result, investment efficiency is higher. Similarly, when the project is “diversifying,” security prices are less informative and, as a result, investment efficiency is lower. This implies that the expected value of diversifying investments will be lower even when the manager’s interests are aligned with shareholders. This channel could, for instance, help explain the findings ofSchoar (2002), who shows that total productivity falls when firms make diversifying (instead of related) investments.32
article.6 Conclusion:
In this paper, we analyze the implications for investment efficiency when the riskiness of a firm’s cash flows is endogenously driven by a novel “feedback loop”: managers’ decision to invest depends on investors’ decision to acquire information. We develop a novel, tractable REE that incorporates this loop between security prices and the firm’s investment decision and allows for nonlinear claims including levered equity. Using this framework, we argue that even in the presence of risky debt, learning from prices generically crowds out some inefficient investment decisions and crowds in some efficient ones, improving investment efficiency. However, investors’ endogenous learning creates an asymmetric effect: the price is more informative for risk-increasing investments (which can give rise to risk shifting) than for risk-decreasing investments (which can give rise to debt overhang). As a result, projects that can give rise to risk shifting are less likely to be adopted, while those subject to debt overhang are more likely to be abandoned.

The model we introduce in this paper also provides a tractable foundation for future research on financial markets when the presence of risky debt is an essential ingredient. For example, in our model, the manager takes as given the firm’s capital structure: specifically, outstanding equity and any previously issued debt. As we show, however, in imperfectly integrated markets, the choice of capital structure influences the quality of the aggregate information available in the economy. This provides a potentially novel motivation for issuing debt: in such settings, the manager will choose the firm’s capital structure to maximize total information production to improve the quality of his investment decisions. We hope to explore these questions in future research.
